event: Early Homo sapiens hunter-gatherers inhabit the Congo Basin, leaving behind the Semliki harpoons (Katanda), among the world's oldest sophisticated bone tools.
Stone Age foraging communities thrive in the equatorial rainforest, the ancestral homeland of Central African Pygmy peoples.
Bantu-speaking peoples migrate into the Congo Basin, introducing agriculture, ironworking, and new languages that reshape the region.
The Upemba Depression in Katanga develops complex iron-working and copper-trading societies, ancestral to later Luba states.
The Kingdom of Kongo is founded by Lukeni lua Nimi near the lower Congo River, becoming a powerful centralized state.
Portuguese explorer Diogo Cão reaches the mouth of the Congo River, opening contact between the Kingdom of Kongo and Europe.
King Nzinga a Nkuwu of Kongo converts to Christianity and is baptized as João I, forging diplomatic ties with Portugal.
Afonso I (Mvemba a Nzinga) becomes king of Kongo, promoting Christianity and literacy while struggling against the growing Atlantic slave trade.
The Kingdom of Luba is consolidated in the Upemba region, establishing a sacred kingship model influential across Central Africa.
Kongo forces are defeated by the Portuguese at the Battle of Mbwila, accelerating the kingdom's fragmentation and decline.
The Lunda Empire expands across the southern savanna, controlling long-distance trade in copper, salt, and slaves.
Swahili-Arab trader Tippu Tip extends a slave- and ivory-trading network deep into the eastern Congo region.
Henry Morton Stanley completes his trans-African expedition by descending the Congo River from its source to the Atlantic.
The Berlin Conference recognizes King Leopold II's claim, and the Congo Free State is established as his personal possession.
Belgian forces conquer the Katanga region, seizing its rich copper and mineral deposits for the Free State.
Forced rubber and ivory extraction under Leopold's regime causes mass atrocities, mutilations, and millions of deaths across the colony.
After international outcry over abuses, the Belgian state annexes the territory, creating the Belgian Congo.
Religious leader Simon Kimbangu launches a prophetic anti-colonial movement before being imprisoned by Belgian authorities.
Anti-colonial riots erupt in Léopoldville, pushing Belgium toward rapid decolonization of the Congo.
The Congo gains independence on 30 June with Patrice Lumumba as prime minister and Joseph Kasavubu as president.
Patrice Lumumba is overthrown and assassinated amid the Congo Crisis and the Katanga secession led by Moïse Tshombe.
The Katanga secession collapses after UN military intervention reintegrates the breakaway province.
Army chief Joseph-Désiré Mobutu seizes power in a coup, beginning a decades-long authoritarian dictatorship.
Mobutu renames the country Zaire and launches his 'authenticité' campaign, africanizing names including his own as Mobutu Sese Seko.
Mobutu nationalizes foreign-owned enterprises under 'Zairianization,' a policy that deepens economic mismanagement.
Kinshasa hosts the 'Rumble in the Jungle' heavyweight bout between Muhammad Ali and George Foreman, a global cultural spectacle.
The First Shaba War sees rebels invade Katanga from Angola before being repelled with foreign assistance.
Unpaid soldiers loot Kinshasa as Zaire's economy collapses and pressure mounts for multiparty reform.
Over a million Rwandan refugees, including genocide perpetrators, flood into eastern Zaire, destabilizing the region.
The First Congo War begins as Laurent-Désiré Kabila's AFDL rebellion, backed by Rwanda and Uganda, advances westward.
Mobutu flees as Laurent-Désiré Kabila captures Kinshasa and renames the country the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
The Second Congo War erupts, drawing in nine African nations in a conflict later called 'Africa's World War.'
Laurent-Désiré Kabila is assassinated and succeeded by his son Joseph Kabila as president.
The Sun City Agreement and Pretoria Accord lay the framework for ending the Second Congo War and forming a transitional government.
A transitional power-sharing government takes office, formally ending the Second Congo War that killed an estimated 5 million people.
The country holds its first multiparty democratic elections in over four decades, confirming Joseph Kabila as president.
Renewed fighting in the eastern Kivu provinces, driven by the CNDP rebellion, displaces hundreds of thousands.
The M23 rebel movement seizes the eastern city of Goma before being driven out the following year.
Long-delayed elections produce the country's first peaceful transfer of power, with Félix Tshisekedi declared the winner.
Félix Tshisekedi is inaugurated as president, succeeding Joseph Kabila after 18 years in power.
A major eruption of Mount Nyiragongo near Goma kills dozens and displaces hundreds of thousands.
Félix Tshisekedi wins re-election as conflict with M23 rebels intensifies in the mineral-rich east.
M23 forces capture Goma and Bukavu in a major eastern offensive, prompting renewed international peace efforts.
